en.extract:
The band-tailed manakin (Pipra fasciicauda) is a species of bird in the family Pipridae. It is a small, frugivorous passerine found in humid montane and lowland tropical forests throughout Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Paraguay, and Peru.


Taxonomy and systematics
The band-tailed manakin has these five subspecies:

P. f. calamae Hellmayr, 1910
P. f. saturata Zimmer, JT, 1936
P. f. purusiana Snethlage, E, 1907
P. f. fasciicauda Hellmayr, 1906
P. f. scarlatina Hellmayr, 1915
The band-tailed manakin forms a superspecies with its two congeners, the wire-tailed manakin (P. filicauda) and crimson-hooded manakin (P. aureola). The species was described to science by Carl Eduard Hellmayr in 1906. Its average generation length is approximately 3.73 years.


Description
The band-tailed manakin is 10 to 11 cm (3.9 to 4.3 in) long and weighs 11.5 to 19 g (0.41 to 0.67 oz). The species is sexually dimorphic. Adult males of the nominate subspecies P. f. fasciicauda have a yellow face and throat; the rest of their head, nape, and upper mantle are red. The rest of their upperparts, wings, and tail are mostly black. They have a white base to the tail and a white patch on the inner webs of the flight feathers that shows as a stripe in flight. The red of the nape  extends onto the breast with some yellow mixed in and the rest of their underparts are light creamy yellow. They have a white or whitish iris. Adult females have a mostly dull olive-green head, upperparts, wings, and tail. Their face and breast have a yellow tinge; their lower breast and belly are grayish that is lightest on the belly. They have a pale gray or magenta to whitish iris. Both sexes have a blackish bill with some pale gray on the mandible, dull reddish legs, and sooty gray feet. Immature males have a brown or gray iris but otherwise resemble adult females. The other subspecies differ little from the nominate. P. f. calamae has slightly more red on its breast, P. f. scarlatina even more and deeper red, and P. f. saturata a nearly entirely red breast and upper belly.
The formative plumage is olive-green overall, with distinct molt limits in the greater coverts. Juvenile feathers are a dull green with some yellow wash. New, larger coverts are a brighter green shade, with denser barbules that protrude 2-3 mm past the old feathers. The iris color ranges from pinkish to white, and the upper mandible of the bill appears gray with a pale base, while the lower mandible of the bill mainly consists of a pale shade.


Distribution and habitat
The band-tailed manakin is a bird of the southern Amazon Basin and the Pantanal. The subspecies are found thus:

P. f. calamae: west-central Brazil on the upper Madeira River and far northwestern Mato Grosso
P. f. saturata: Huallaga River watershed in northern Peru's Department of San Martín
P. f. purusiana: from eastern Peru between Loreto and Cuzco departments east into western Brazil's Acre and western Amazonas states
P. f. fasciicauda: from Madre de Dios and northern Puno departments in southeastern Peru east into Bolivia's northern Santa Cruz Department
P. f. scarlatina: central Brazil roughly bounded by central Pará, western Mato Grosso do Sul, southern Goiás, western Minas Gerais, and northwestern São Paulo states extending into far northern Bolivia's Pando and Beni departments and far northeastern Argentina's Misiones Province; also isolated populations in northeastern Brazil's Ceará and Alagoas states.
The band-tailed manakin inhabits lowland várzea and gallery forests. In elevation it reaches 600 m (2,000 ft) in Brazil and 1,000 m (3,300 ft) in Peru.


Behavior


Movement
The band-tailed manakin is believed to be a year-round resident; no movements have been documented.


Feeding
Band-tailed manakins are frugivorous passerines, though they have been recorded briefly following swarms of Eciton burchelli army ants. In a study in South Brazil (Mato Grosso do Sul), 93% of band-tailed manakin fecal samples were composed of fruits and seeds, with the remaining 7% being insect remains.


Breeding
The band-tailed manakin breeds mostly between August and November in southern Brazil and somewhat later in the north. Males make a complex courtship display flight to females in a lek but only the dominant male mates. The leks are often in the dense forest understory. The species' nest is a tiny open cup made from fungal rhizomorphs; it is typically built on a base of dead leaves in a branch fork about 2 to 3 m (7 to 10 ft) up in a small tree. The clutch is two eggs. The incubation period, time to fledging, and details of parental care are not known.


Vocal and non-vocal sounds
Males at a lek make "a downward-inflected eeew [and] a somewhat harsh wee-ee-eeh" calls. During the flight display they make "a prolonged eeeooo" call. Their wings make a "very low-pitched kloop" during the display flight and a "sharp klok" when they land.


Hybridization
Studies of plumage coloration and genetic variation have investigated hybridization and introgression between the band-tailed manakin and its sister species P. fasciicauda and P. aureola. Research suggests that hybridization in Amazonian birds may occur due to the slow evolution of reproductive isolation, allowing even taxa that diverged millions of years ago to hybridize upon contact.


Status
The IUCN has assessed the band-tailed manakin as being of Least Concern. It has a very large range; it population size is not known and is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified. It is considered common in Brazil and fairly common in Peru. It occurs in several protected areas.


References

 Media related to Pipra fasciicauda at Wikimedia Commons

The '''band-tailed manakin''' ('''''Pipra fasciicauda''''') is a species of bird in the family [[Pipridae]]. It is a small, [[Frugivore|frugivorous]] [[passerine]] found in humid [[Montane ecosystem|montane]] and lowland tropical forests<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Snow |first=David |date=2020 |title=Band-tailed Manakin (Pipra fasciicauda), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/batman1/cur/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.batman1.01 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref> throughout [[Argentina]], [[Bolivia]], [[Brazil]], [[Paraguay]], and [[Peru]].<ref name=IOC15.1>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/cotingas/ |title=Cotingas, manakins, tityras, becards |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 15.1 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=March 2025 |access-date=3 March 2025 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==
The band-tailed manakin has these five subspecies:<ref name=IOC15.1/>
*''P. f. calamae'' {{au|[[Carl Eduard Hellmayr|Hellmayr]], 1910}}
*''P. f. saturata'' {{au|[[John T. Zimmer|Zimmer, JT]], 1936}}
*''P. f. purusiana'' {{au|[[Emilie Snethlage|Snethlage, E]], 1907}}
*''P. f. fasciicauda'' {{au|Hellmayr, 1906}}
*''P. f. scarlatina'' {{au|Hellmayr, 1915}}

The band-tailed manakin forms a [[superspecies]] with its two [[Congener (biology)|congeners]], the [[wire-tailed manakin]] (''P. filicauda'') and [[crimson-hooded manakin]] (''P. aureola'').<ref name=SACClist>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, D. F. Lane, L, N. Naka, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. Version 29 September 2025. A classification of the bird species of South America. South American Classification Committee associated with the International Ornithological Union. https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCBaseline.htm retrieved September 29, 2025</ref> The species was described to science by Carl Eduard Hellmayr in 1906.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Pipra fasciicauda fasciicauda (Band-tailed Manakin (fasciicauda)) - Avibase |url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?avibaseid=43EDDC0B&utm_ |access-date=2025-10-14 |website=avibase.bsc-eoc.org}}</ref> Its average generation length is approximately 3.73 years.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=BirdLife International (BirdLife International) |date=2024-06-12 |title=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species: Pipra fasciicauda |url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/22700988/263825628 |journal=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250429061338/https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/22700988/263825628 |archive-date=2025-04-29 |access-date=2025-10-14 |url-status=live }}</ref>

==Description==
The band-tailed manakin is {{convert|10|to|11|cm|in|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} long and weighs {{convert|11.5|to|19|g|oz|sigfig=2|abbr=on}}. The species is [[sexually dimorphic]]. Adult males of the [[nominate subspecies]] ''P. f. fasciicauda'' have a yellow face and throat; the rest of their head, nape, and upper mantle are red. The rest of their upperparts, wings, and tail are mostly black. They have a white base to the tail and a white patch on the inner webs of the flight feathers that shows as a stripe in flight. The red of the nape  extends onto the breast with some yellow mixed in and the rest of their underparts are light creamy yellow. They have a white or whitish iris. Adult females have a mostly dull olive-green head, upperparts, wings, and tail. Their face and breast have a yellow tinge; their lower breast and belly are grayish that is lightest on the belly. They have a pale gray or magenta to whitish iris. Both sexes have a blackish bill with some pale gray on the [[mandible]], dull reddish legs, and sooty gray feet. Immature males have a brown or gray iris but otherwise resemble adult females. The other subspecies differ little from the nominate. ''P. f. calamae'' has slightly more red on its breast, ''P. f. scarlatina'' even more and deeper red, and ''P. f. saturata'' a nearly entirely red breast and upper belly.<ref name=BTMA-BOW>Snow, D. (2020). Band-tailed Manakin (''Pipra fasciicauda''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.batman1.01 retrieved October 5, 2025</ref>

The formative [[plumage]] is olive-green overall, with distinct molt limits in the greater coverts. Juvenile feathers are a dull green with some yellow wash. New, larger coverts are a brighter green shade, with denser [[mwod:barbule|barbules]] that protrude 2-3 mm past the old feathers. The iris color ranges from pinkish to white, and the upper [[Mandible (arthropod mouthpart)|mandible]] of the bill appears gray with a pale base, while the lower [[Mandible (arthropod mouthpart)|mandible]] of the bill mainly consists of a pale shade.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Sampaio |first=Larissa |last2=Ferraz |first2=Dnilson Oliveira |last3=da Costa |first3=Ana Carolina Moreira |last4=Aleixo |first4=Alexandre |last5=Cerqueira |first5=Pablo Vieira |last6=Araripe |first6=Juliana |last7=do Rêgo |first7=Péricles Sena |date=2020-04-01 |title=Analyses of plumage coloration and genetic variation confirm the hybridization of ''Pipra fasciicauda'' and ''Pipra aureola'' in the Brazilian Amazon basin |url=https://doi.org/10.1007/s10336-020-01744-1 |journal=Journal of Ornithology |language=en |volume=161 |issue=2 |pages=503–508 |doi=10.1007/s10336-020-01744-1 |issn=2193-7206|hdl=10138/324747 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
The band-tailed manakin is a bird of the southern [[Amazon Basin]] and the [[Pantanal]]. The subspecies are found thus:<ref name=IOC15.1/><ref name=BTMA-BOW/><ref name=vanPerlo>{{cite book | last =van Perlo | first = Ber| title =A Field Guide to the Birds of Brazil | publisher =Oxford University Press | date =2009 | location =New York | pages =278–279 | isbn =978-0-19-530155-7 }}</ref>

*''P. f. calamae'': west-central Brazil on the upper [[Madeira River]] and far northwestern [[Mato Grosso]]
*''P. f. saturata'': [[Huallaga River]] watershed in northern Peru's [[Department of San Martín]]<ref name=Schulenberg>{{cite book | last =Schulenberg | first =T.S. | last2 =Stotz | first2 =D.F. | last3 =Lane | first3 =D.F. | last4 =O'Neill | first4 =J.P. | last5 =Parker | first5 =T.A. III | title =Birds of Peru | publisher =Princeton University Press | edition =revised and updated |series=Princeton Field Guides | date =2010 | location =Princeton, NJ | pages =504  |isbn = 978-0691130231 }}</ref>
*''P. f. purusiana'': from eastern Peru between [[Department of Loreto|Loreto]] and [[Department of Cusco|Cuzco]] departments east into western Brazil's [[Acre (state)|Acre]] and western [[Amazonas (Brazilian state)|Amazonas]] states<ref name=Schulenberg/>
*''P. f. fasciicauda'': from [[Department of Madre de Dios|Madre de Dios]] and northern [[Department of Puno|Puno]] departments in southeastern Peru east into Bolivia's northern [[Santa Cruz Department]]<ref name=Schulenberg/>
*''P. f. scarlatina'': central Brazil roughly bounded by central [[Pará]], western [[Mato Grosso do Sul]], southern [[Goiás]], western [[Minas Gerais]], and northwestern [[São Paulo (state)|São Paulo]] states extending into far northern Bolivia's [[Pando Department|Pando]] and [[Beni Department|Beni]] departments and far northeastern Argentina's [[Misiones Province]]; also isolated populations in northeastern Brazil's [[Ceará]] and [[Alagoas]] states.<ref name=Peña>{{cite book | last =de la Peña | first =Martín R. | last2=Rumboll |first2=Maurice|  title =Birds of Southern South America and Antarctica | publisher =Princeton University Press | series =Princeton Illustrated Checklists | date =2001 | location =New Jersey | pages =Plate 81, map 81.7 | isbn =0691090351 }}</ref>

The band-tailed manakin inhabits lowland ''[[Várzea forest|várzea]]'' and [[Gallery forest|gallery]] forests.<ref name=BTMA-BOW/><ref name=vanPerlo/><ref name=Schulenberg/> In elevation it reaches {{convert|600|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Brazil and {{convert|1000|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Peru.<ref name=vanPerlo/><ref name=Schulenberg/>

==Behavior==

===Movement===
The band-tailed manakin is believed to be a year-round resident; no movements have been documented.<ref name=BTMA-BOW/>

===Feeding===
Band-tailed manakins are [[Frugivore|frugivorous]] [[passerine]]s,<ref>{{Cite journal |last=BirdLife International (BirdLife International) |date=2024-06-12 |title=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species: ''Pipra fasciicauda'' |url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/22700988/263825628 |journal=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250429061338/https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/22700988/263825628 |archive-date=2025-04-29 |access-date=2025-10-14 |url-status=live }}</ref> though they have been recorded briefly following swarms of ''[[Eciton burchelli]]'' [[army ant]]s.<ref name="BTMA-BOW" /> In a study in South Brazil ([[Mato Grosso do Sul]]), 93% of band-tailed manakin [[Feces|fecal]] samples were composed of fruits and seeds, with the remaining 7% being insect remains'''.'''<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Snow |first=David |date=2020 |title=Band-tailed Manakin (''Pipra fasciicauda''), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/batman1/cur/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.batman1.01 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

===Breeding===
The band-tailed manakin breeds mostly between August and November in southern Brazil and somewhat later in the north.<ref name=BTMA-BOW/> Males make a complex courtship display flight to females in a [[Lek mating|lek]] but only the dominant male mates. The leks are often in the dense forest understory.<ref name=BTMA-BOW/><ref name=Schulenberg/> The species' nest is a tiny open cup made from fungal [[rhizomorph]]s; it is typically built on a base of dead leaves in a branch fork about {{convert|2|to|3|m|ft|0|abbr=on}} up in a small tree. The clutch is two eggs. The [[incubation period]], time to [[Fledge|fledging]], and details of parental care are not known.<ref name=BTMA-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Pipra-fasciicauda |species=the band-tailed manakin}}

===Vocal and non-vocal sounds===
Males at a lek make "a downward-inflected ''eeew'' [and] a somewhat harsh ''wee-ee-eeh''" calls. During the flight display they make "a prolonged ''eeeooo''" call. Their wings make a "very low-pitched ''kloop''" during the display flight and a "sharp ''klok''" when they land.<ref name=BTMA-BOW/>

=== Hybridization ===
Studies of plumage coloration and genetic variation have investigated hybridization and introgression between the band-tailed manakin and its sister species ''P.{{nbsp}}fasciicauda'' and [[Crimson-hooded manakin|''P.{{nbsp}}aureola'']]. Research suggests that hybridization in Amazonian birds may occur due to the slow evolution of reproductive isolation, allowing even taxa that diverged millions of years ago to hybridize upon contact.<ref name=":0" />

==Status==
The [[IUCN]] has assessed the band-tailed manakin as being of Least Concern. It has a very large range; it population size is not known and is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified.<ref name=IUCN/> It is considered common in Brazil and fairly common in Peru.<ref name=vanPerlo/><ref name=Schulenberg/> It occurs in several protected areas.<ref name=BTMA-BOW/>
